speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Regina Horowitz, H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z. And this is roll 2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue. We were talking about when the Poles were throwing water on the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. By the time we finished our work that day, by the time we reached our homes, we found out that those 10 Jews were hung anyway. [SIGHS] I recall that Erev Yom Kippur, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my parents were praying on the top floor, on the third-- third floor in our building, in somebody's house. I mean, not only our-- my parents, but all the Jews, all the people. And the young ones, we were doing vigil. We were watching. I had the privilege of having to watch the door, the main door, outside of the building, downstairs, before the curfew started, before it was 5-- 6:30. And I see the Gestapo cars pulling over to the second-- to the corner down the street. And I saw they took out a young man and a young woman. That was Gusta Davidson-- her maiden name was Davidovich-- and her husband. She was a very active Zionist in Akiba. And they had her on the list. I don't think she was ever released from prison, or her husband. But she wrote a book on Montelupich, which was a prison. She was kept there for a long time. And she used to throw through the window pieces of paper. She wrote a book, [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means 'The Diary of Justyna.' She wrote that book. And people were, whoever had those pieces of paper-- I think that I read the book after liberation from these pieces of paper that she was throwing out. And then we closed the door. I closed the door. And when I retold that story to my sisters, my-- Gusta went to school with my sister who was just before me. And we just could not believe. But this was the first experience with people being taken, or people being interned for-- for being Jews. And later on came more Aktions and came more, the burning of the beards, the-- the dancing of-- pull out the-- from their own homes, pulling out the Jews that prayed. Pulling them out and burning of their payos and their beards, or making them dance. And they had the show. The Pole-- the Germans started the show, and the Poles were continuing with it, stirring them up. And it was just a horrible thing to see, horrible sight. And I, myself, us, ourselves, we could not do anything about it. I'll never forget one day I walked into-- because I left early to bring some fresh butter to the house, to buy butter, to buy whatever I could, bread. And I came home, and a-- a man is sitting at the table, eating, in my father's seat, having his breakfast. I felt the nose is familiar, and so are the eyes. But that face is not my father's. Something is different. My father shaved off his beard. Because he felt if he wants to go to work-- at that time, he was still allowed to work-- so he felt if he wants to go through the streets to go to work not to be attacked, stopped, he might as well do that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was such a horrible shock for me, not to be able to recognize my own father. I just stood there like, and tears were rolling down my face, and so were tears down his face. He says, my child doesn't recognize me. I says, because all my life for 16 years, I've known you with a beard. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those are the little things that made life quite interesting. And slowly the noose was getting tighter around our necks. You had to give up radio. You had to-- you had no-- you were not allowed to have a telephone. You were not allowed to have a radio. You were not allowed to have a fur coat. You were not allowed to have a fur collar. You were not allowed to have [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] certain necessities, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the fur coats and the radios?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to bring voluntary to an-- an area that they recommend, that they specified, were to deposit. And then came the time where you, if you had a bank account, it wasn't yours anymore. If you had a few zlotys you had to give it up, and they exchange it for pittance and the deutsche marks. Let's say if you went with 500 zlotys to exchange, you got the worst of 50 zlotys in the deutsche marks. They gave you 500, but that was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Poles have to do that too, or just the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just the Jews. Just the Jew, not the Poles. You see, the Poles were treated yet in a different manner. Whoever had a nice apartment, and I'm speaking of luxury apartments, they had to not to give up completely. But they had to go into one room, the whole family, and to give the rest of the apartment for a German [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] officer. If you had a maid, you had to let her go. And you had to be-- become the maid for that officer. For this, he allowed you to stay in-- in that one room for the whole family. That was the normal routine. That was the norm. And that was all right. We accepted that. All right, we did not have a big apartment, so by us, no. But they did take out our furniture. They came, and they took the furniture. And yes, on the 1st of December, prior to allowing-- no, that was in November of '39-- they closed off the Jewish quarters. And the looting and robbing, for 36 hours, went on endlessly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the looting and robbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Nazis. They closed off the streets, the tramways that passed through the main streets of the Jewish quarters. No one had-- was allowed to get on. No one was allowed to get off in that-- passing from that area. And I recall seven of them walked into our home, into our apartment, with the guns drawing. And if you made the wrong move, bingo, you were shot. Fortunately, in-- at that time in our building, nobody-- everybody cooperated with them and whatever there was. I recall they had pillowcases, my mother's pillowcases, they took. And they were throwing candlesticks, and candelabra, and whatever else that was worthwhile in silver. And then they started looking for gold. They made my mother undress to remove her girdle. And they stopped with that girdle, and they were going over if there is anything hidden, whether dollars are hidden there, or some jewelry. I'll never forget that experience. It just was a horrendous feeling, especially my poor mother, when they made her undressed, and she objected. And they said, I'm going to kill you if you don't do it. And we said, and my father said, do it. We knew she has nothing on her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But nonetheless, not-- nothing would have stopped them from killing her. They did it to every woman in the building, that they had to undress and hand it over to them, their girdle, their bra, whatever, if anything is hidden there. But from my mother's house, they took on maybe 50 kilo of silver plus good linens. I guess the porcelain they did not take because it just would have break. This would break. Whatever else they were, they took.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 36 hours of that. We did not know if we're allowed to sleep, if we're allowed to lie down, and whatever else. Were we allowed to put the lights on or not? We did not know what to do until we saw the trucks pulling away with the loot. And it was just like nothing after that. There-- there-- there was the money, with the armband, with this, with that. It became-- we were all dying slowly. What armband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Armbands with the star of David. We wore armbands, white with blue star of David. We had to make sure that on the 1st of December, each member of the-- of every Jewish family had that on them. And if you dared walk outside without it, you could have been shot. You could have been killed. You could have been imprisoned.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, my sisters were blue-eyed blondes. And they went to work. And the supervisor, the-- the German guy, who-- said to my sister, my oldest sister, he says, mensch, [GERMAN]. Hey, man, what did you do? Woman, what did you do? Why did you put it on? And she said to him, I am Jewish. Ich bin Jude.

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]. Impossible that you should be a Jew. You are just working with them. And she says, no, I'm Jewish. With a name like yours, you can-- and with the face of-- like yours, you could get away without it. And he says, I'm not going to jeopardize my life. Now, she still worked for a while. She was allowed to. I mean, all my sisters worked. And slowly my father's firms were taken over by the Germans. And he was not allowed-- and I mean then he was not rehired by the German overseers, and he became unemployed. And as such, he had to register. And natural my mother was never employed. So she had to register with her husband. And so was his mother. And I was underage. So they had the four of us on the list. Therefore, some time in November, we received-- of '40, 1940, we had received white cards written that on that and that a day, I still remember, on December 8, 1940, with what-- whatever you can carry on you, whatever you could carry with you, come voluntarily to the station for resettlement. Now, we each read those cards, and we knew that's no good. They gave us a few weeks' time. And we started thinking of what to do. Being our-- my-- all the sisters were working, they did not have them on their list, the decision was that we going to run away to a smaller town. And my parents made the arrangements with a Pole who was rewarded for his-- for-- for staking out his life. He bought tickets for the train, and we went to Tarnow. And it's true, I had my parents about a year and a half longer. We still stayed as a family. Meanwhile, there began talk about a ghetto in Krakow. And my sisters liquidated the-- the home as well as they could, some things if they were able to sell, if they are able to give away. Or whatever that man that transported us to Tarnow, some of the things he took and brought to us, mainly clothing and whatever. And then he bought tickets for my sisters, and they joined us. And we were-- were again a family. My grandmother's sister and brother lived in Tarnow. That's why we chose that town. But apartments were scarce, because there were influx of people from different little towns that were made Judenfrei or whatever. People kept on running from big city to small city, from a small town to a bigger town. It was just like the Tower of Babel. And we stayed together with my mother's-- with my grandmother's sister. And again, my uncle had to register. His underage son had to register, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother, my father. They did not ask for my grandmother. And we did not register her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- at that time, I think I turned 18 already-- or did I work? I don't remember. I think I worked already, so I had some piece of paper, an identification that I worked. And they had a list. So they formed in Tarnow the Jewish police. We heard rumors [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that Warsaw ghetto is being-- people are being shipped out of Warsaw ghetto. We heard rumors that transports went out from Krakow toward Przeworsk. That was a town in the eastern part of Galicia. And we did not know what transpired, but that people were transported in cattle cars. This we heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally a day-- means-- a few days prior to the first Aktion in Tarnow, they looked-- they took 100 young men, Jewish men, and they named them the Jewish police, [GERMAN]. They gave them caps. And they had the privilege, they had the lists, and they had the privilege of going to addresses whom to take. With my parents and my relatives, they were still nice. They came 3 o'clock in the morning, knocked on the door. And they said, by 6 o'clock, I'll come back for you. I'm giving you time to say goodbye, or pack up whatever you can. And I'll never forget. My father came over to me. We had knapsacks. And he came over to me, oh, you pack for me. I don't need anything except my tallit and my kittel. That's the shroud. He says, that's all I need. He said to me, that's all I use. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [? that ?] [? was-- ?] and my father gave me that night-- was in those three hours after I made his package and my mother's. Because my sisters were numb, I was the one that had to take over. I don't know how-- where I got that strength from. But somehow I did that. And then my father handed me a cigarette. He says, maybe it will calm you down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I smoked for long time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Auschwitz, I gave my bread for one day for two drags on a cigarette. We shared, a few girls shared, the cigarette. And I gave away my-- but I never did it again. [LAUGHS] And-- [SIGHS] I still see my parents walking down the street. You weren't allowed to look, because you had to put black covers over the windows. You were not allowed, but I peeked out. And I saw my parents walking down the street holding hands. And I remember my grandmother saying, child, you have no right to cry. You saw your people-- your parents, walking out on their own two feet. So you have no right to cry. Only if somebody is carried, that's when you cry. She didn't let us cry. And she didn't cry either. She just took the-- her prayer book, which was-- that was her Bible. And she just sat down and read whatever there was from-- from place to place. She knew it all by heart, praying for all these people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was on a Thursday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was June 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. On Monday, there was another Aktion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we only saw trucks going with dead bodies through, passing through the streets. We did not know what went on. My sisters, two of them, had the papers that we working. My oldest sister and my grandmother hid in a bunker with other people of the building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They came into the apartment, and never mind the paper, we need you. And this was their sport. They lined us up, whomever they were able to scrounge around. Paper or no paper in your hand, didn't matter. They lined us against the wall. And they felt they're going to have a sport. They are going to shoot the 10th one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the Jewish police, or was this the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Jewish police did not carry guns. I'm speaking now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the Nazis. The Jewish police did their job the few days prior to that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Afterwards, we-- we will get to that, what the Jewish police had to do later on. But meanwhile, the Nazis came around. That was their sport. They lined us up. The 10th one will be shot. Now, I was standing, my two sisters over my side. And I was the 10th one. They had 66 people lined up. And as they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are aiming, somebody came from the marketplace, screaming, get them. We need these people for transport. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we marching through the-- and there is [? Slovak ?] people kneeling. And they make us right away kneel down with the head down. And all of a sudden, I see a pair of reddish, brownish boots. I'll never forget that color, like a maroon leather boot. And he gives me a kick in my knee. And he says-- he looks at my paper. And he says, [GERMAN]. Run home. And I said, [GERMAN]. These are my sisters. [SPEAKING GERMAN] The three of you, [GERMAN]. Run home, the three of you. By the time we reach the corner of the marketplace into a street, that marketplace was empty from people. Nobody was left there. And we went home. And all of a sudden, we see blood is running. Blood is running through the gutters. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And this is when we realized the Jewish police rounded up the-- some people. They had to round up some people. At that-- that time, there was 4,000 people at transport on Thursday. 4,000 people were shot on Thursday. And the Jewish police was very busy burying them at the cemetery, therefore the trucks with the dead bodies. [? The other day ?] another 4,000 people at transport, and a few thousand more killed. And then on Thursday was another transport. They cleaned out Tarnow that-- [? that was in ?] those three Aktion of 24,000 people, Jews. And after that, they formed a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was that person who told you and your sisters to run quickly?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was one of the Nazis. I don't know his name. But I swear that every Aktion that I saw him. He was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea why he told you to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. Maybe he felt I'm young. Maybe I-- I don't know. Whatever it was, whatever-- I could swear that every Aktion that I had seen him. And I think even on the transport to Auschwitz from-- from Plaszow, I think he was there also at the train station. Must have been a big wheel. He was a big, heavyset guy. I never looked at his face because I was told not to ever look at them. But I still remember those boots. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, we got into the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The head of the police, his wife, the head of the man who married, his wife went to school with my oldest sister. And when she found out that we are there, that they took our parents away-- they had an apartment, and she did not want strangers in their-- her apartment. So she asked the four of us to live with her. All right. He couldn't have done anything for us because he couldn't do anything for himself. He couldn't do anything for anybody. I don't blame him for anything. But at least we knew we have a clean bed to sleep in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and a room where to be. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I begin to work at Madritsch.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Or was it at-- no. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I worked at a factory that made shirts and underwear. That was not Madritsch yet. That was still a private place which was in business for that reason. I mean, the business belonged to the Germans, but whoever ran it, ran it that way. And I worked there, for I had the paper with the stamp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and my sisters did, too. Now my oldest sister got a job in a place that made brushes. She became a brush maker. So she got her papers. The four of us had papers for a while. Then came September 11, 1942. And again, everybody out. Everybody out. The Jewish police, the Nazis, everybody to the-- to the marketplace. This was a different market, because the other one was already in the non-Jewish section. We got there, and sits a German person. The Nazis are sitting by tables. So you pass them by. And he grabbed my paper, gave me a different stamp on it. And I-- I used again the same tactic, these are-- [GERMAN]. And he-- and before I turned around, my sisters were dragged away. He gave me that, and his aide, or whoever it was, he wore-- he did not wear the green uniform. He wore a black uniform and had that dog. And he looks at me and says, [GERMAN], and took a walk with his dog, and let him go on the leash to [GERMAN], you know, to-- that-- that I had to run home as fast as I could, because otherwise the dog would jump at me. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop for a moment now to change the tape.
